title: Visualization and data storytelling
summary: Match chart types to analytical questions and communicate the real signal without misleading scales or clutter.

keywords: RANDOM WALKS AND MORE ABOUT DATA VISUALIZATION

items.prompt: When is a scatter plot a better choice than a bar chart?

solution.summary: Use a scatter plot when you want to inspect the relationship between two numeric variables rather than compare a few category totals.

hints: Look for the choice that distorts magnitude perception.
items.prompt: Which choice is most likely to mislead a reader?

choices.label: Truncating an axis without clearly signaling the break.
Choosing a chart title that names the takeaway.

solution.summary: Truncated axes can exaggerate apparent differences, especially when the break is not made explicit.

items.prompt: Why are annotations often useful on a chart?

solution.summary: Annotations help the reader focus on the important event, outlier, or pattern instead of guessing what matters.

items.prompt: Create a line chart of the monthly values below using matplotlib and title it `Monthly Active Users`.
items.starterCode:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar']
values = [120, 135, 150]
# create the plot here

pyodide.tests:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
ns = {}
exec(user_code, ns)
fig = plt.gcf()
ax = fig.axes[0]
assert ax.get_title() == 'Monthly Active Users'
assert len(ax.lines) == 1
__grade_result__ = {'passed': True, 'message': 'Your chart has the expected title and a single line.'}

solution.summary: Use the reference solution to compare structure, not just syntax.
solution.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
months = ['Jan', 'Feb', 'Mar']
values = [120, 135, 150]
plt.plot(months, values)
plt.title('Monthly Active Users')
plt.xlabel('Month')
plt.ylabel('Users')
plt.gcf()
